content.text: A persona is a research-grounded archetype of a user — a fictional but representative character that keeps the team aligned on who they are building for. For GenAI applications, the standard persona template is insufficient. It captures demographics, goals, and frustrations but misses the dimensions that drive AI feature design: how much the user understands AI, how much they trust it, what they do when it is wrong, and whether a model error causes inconvenience or serious harm. AI user personas add these dimensions explicitly, turning the persona from a marketing artefact into a design constraint.

attrs.alt: AI Persona Card showing standard dimensions (left) and GenAI-specific dimensions (right): AI Literacy, Trust Calibration, Error Tolerance, Explainability Need; plus Risk & Harm Profile and 5-level AI Literacy Scale

content.text: Why Standard Personas Fall Short for GenAI

A standard persona might describe a user as 'Maya, 34, mid-level analyst at a financial services firm who needs to produce reports faster'. This is useful for a data visualisation tool. For a GenAI-powered report assistant, it misses:
Does Maya know the AI can hallucinate numbers?

If she does not, she will publish AI-generated figures without verification. If the persona does not capture her AI literacy level, the product team will not know whether to add inline source citations, confidence indicators, or mandatory review checkpoints.
What does Maya do when the AI is wrong?

If she over-trusts the output and a compliance report contains a fabricated regulatory citation, the consequence is a regulatory finding. If she under-trusts and fact-checks every sentence, the time saving disappears. Her error tolerance profile determines the guardrail design.
Is Maya in a high-stakes domain?

Financial services regulatory reporting is a domain where an AI error has material legal consequences. The persona's risk profile determines whether the feature should include mandatory human-review gates or is appropriate for autonomous operation.

AI literacy is the most foundational GenAI-specific persona dimension because it determines the entire interaction design: what the UI explains, what controls are exposed, and what trust signals are necessary.
Has never interacted with an AI tool; may not know what LLMs are; likely has strong preconceptions (either fear or magic-thinking)
Maximum guardrails; no jargon; results framed as 'suggestions'; mandatory review before action; progressive disclosure of AI involvement
Knows AI exists and has seen headlines; has used ChatGPT or similar casually 1–2 times; no mental model of why AI fails
Brief onboarding explaining what the AI does and doesn't do; clear 'AI generated' label; prominent feedback button; simple error messages
Uses AI tools daily; has a mental model of prompting; knows hallucination exists; has developed personal review habits
Advanced controls available but not required; streaming output with source citations; tone/length controls; export to verify
Writes prompts; has used APIs or no-code AI builders; understands temperature, context windows; familiar with RAG
Power controls exposed; prompt editor; model parameter visibility; API access option; raw JSON output mode
Trains or fine-tunes models; understands tokenisation, embeddings, attention; can read evals
Full technical exposure; log probability display; eval harness integration; custom model routing; no 'for your protection' guardrails that block power users

A single product may have personas at multiple literacy levels — e.g. a code-review tool used by both junior developers (Aware/User) and senior ML engineers (Builder/Expert). The product must serve both without the advanced controls overwhelming the beginner or the guardrails frustrating the expert. Persona-based feature flagging (showing different UI layers based on a user's stated or inferred literacy) is the typical solution.
Trust calibration describes how well a user's confidence in AI output matches the AI's actual accuracy. Three profiles exist, and all three create product design problems:
Accepts AI output without verification; does not notice errors; may amplify AI mistakes downstream
Mandatory source citations; inline confidence signals; verification checkpoints for high-stakes actions; 'are you sure?' friction for irreversible actions
Spot-checks AI output; verifies claims in high-stakes areas; comfortable skipping verification for low-stakes tasks
Low — this is the target mental model
Reinforce calibration with accurate confidence signals; don't erode trust with unnecessary guardrails
Distrusts all AI output regardless of accuracy; re-does every task manually; abandons the feature after one error
Low for safety, high for adoption
Progressive trust-building: start with low-stakes tasks; show accuracy track record; allow the user to compare AI vs their own output; provide easy override
Trust calibration is not fixed — it shifts over time as users gain experience with a specific AI feature. A persona card should note the
target calibration after 30 days of use
, which informs the progressive onboarding design.

Error tolerance is the user's emotional and procedural response to an AI mistake. The harm profile is the consequence severity of that mistake in the user's specific context. Together they determine the required guardrail intensity.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class ErrorTolerance(str, Enum):
    HIGH   = "high"    # Treats errors as learning opportunities; does not abandon feature
    MEDIUM = "medium"  # Frustrated by errors but continues with verification
    LOW    = "low"     # A single significant error may cause feature abandonment

class HarmSeverity(str, Enum):
    NEGLIGIBLE   = "negligible"   # Incorrect answer to a trivia question
    MINOR        = "minor"        # Wasted time; social embarrassment
    MODERATE     = "moderate"     # Financial cost; reputational damage
    SIGNIFICANT  = "significant"  # Regulatory finding; employment consequence
    SEVERE       = "severe"       # Health impact; legal liability; personal safety

@dataclass
class AIPersona:
    # Standard fields
    name: str
    role: str
    organisation_type: str
    primary_goal: str
    key_frustrations: list[str]

    # GenAI-specific fields
    ai_literacy_level: int          # 0=None, 1=Aware, 2=User, 3=Builder, 4=Expert
    trust_calibration: str          # "over" | "calibrated" | "under"
    error_tolerance: ErrorTolerance
    explainability_need: str        # "high" | "medium" | "low"
    harm_severity: HarmSeverity
    is_vulnerable_user: bool = False   # Protected class, minor, vulnerable adult
    is_adversarial_risk: bool = False  # Could intentionally try to misuse the AI

    def required_guardrail_level(self) -> str:
        """Derive minimum guardrail intensity from harm severity and trust calibration."""
        if self.harm_severity in {HarmSeverity.SEVERE, HarmSeverity.SIGNIFICANT}:
            return "mandatory_human_review"
        if self.harm_severity == HarmSeverity.MODERATE:
            if self.trust_calibration == "over":
                return "verification_checkpoint"
            return "source_citations_required"
        return "standard_confidence_signals"

    def ui_tier(self) -> str:
        """Which UI layer to show by default."""
        if self.ai_literacy_level <= 1:  return "guided"
        if self.ai_literacy_level <= 2:  return "standard"
        if self.ai_literacy_level == 3:  return "advanced"
        return "expert"

    def summary(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"{self.name} ({self.role}): "
            f"AI literacy={self.ai_literacy_level}, "
            f"trust={self.trust_calibration}, "
            f"harm={self.harm_severity.value}, "
            f"guardrail={self.required_guardrail_level()}, "
            f"ui={self.ui_tier()}"
        )

# Example personas for a GenAI legal document review tool
maya = AIPersona(
    name="Maya Chen",
    role="Junior Associate Solicitor",
    organisation_type="Mid-size law firm",
    primary_goal="Review contract clauses faster without missing risk terms",
    key_frustrations=["Reviewing 50-page contracts in 2 hours", "Partner reviewing turnaround"],
    ai_literacy_level=1,      # Aware — has used ChatGPT but not in legal context
    trust_calibration="over",  # Likely to trust AI legal output without checking
    error_tolerance=ErrorTolerance.LOW,      # One AI error causing client complaint = abandonment
    explainability_need="high",              # Needs to know WHICH clause and WHY it is risky
    harm_severity=HarmSeverity.SIGNIFICANT,  # Missing a risky clause → legal liability
    is_vulnerable_user=False,
)

print(maya.summary())
# Maya Chen (Junior Associate Solicitor): AI literacy=1, trust=over, harm=significant,
# guardrail=mandatory_human_review, ui=guided

Explainability need captures how much of the AI's reasoning the user needs to see to take action and trust the output. This dimension is independent of literacy level — a high-literacy user may still need full explanations (because they are accountable for the output), while a low-literacy user may need a simple binary answer.
Will not act on output without understanding the basis; may be accountable to a regulator or manager for the decision
Cited sources inline; expandable reasoning chain; confidence score per claim; 'how did you get this?' button
Spot-checks explanations for surprising outputs; trusts routine outputs without explanation
Collapsed 'show reasoning' toggle; source footnotes on hover; summary confidence indicator
Just wants the answer; finds explanations distracting or overwhelming; will verify through other means if outcome is wrong
Clean output with no inline metadata; optional 'show sources' link at the bottom; no confidence scores unless output is low-confidence

High-explainability users and low-explainability users often coexist in the same product (e.g. a customer support tool used by both agents and supervisors). Design for progressive disclosure: low-explainability as the default, with explanations available on-demand for users who need them. Do not force explanations on users who find them distracting — it degrades adoption.
Two boolean flags on the persona card trigger additional design requirements that standard product development misses entirely:
A persona is flagged as vulnerable if they belong to a group that is at elevated risk of harm from AI errors or AI manipulation. Categories include:
Mental health crisis users interacting with a support chatbot.
Financial distress users interacting with a credit or debt management tool.
Minors using any consumer AI feature.
Elderly users with lower digital literacy in a scam-prone context.
Non-native speakers where language nuance may cause AI misinterpretation.

When the vulnerable flag is set, the feature must: include signposting to human support; apply additional content filters; not use persuasive design patterns (urgency, scarcity); and trigger a human review escalation path for any output that touches the vulnerability area.
An adversarial persona is one that

attempts to misuse the AI — through prompt injection, jailbreaking, extracting training data, or generating policy-violating content. Adversarial personas are distinct from genuine users who accidentally trigger edge cases. When the adversarial flag is set:
A red-team persona card must be created in parallel with the standard persona card.
The red-team persona's attack scenarios must be documented and used to generate test cases for the guardrail evaluation suite.
The hotspot design spike for any AI Decision node that the adversarial persona could reach must include adversarial test coverage as a definition-of-done criterion.
Building AI Personas from Research Data

GenAI personas should be grounded in empirical research, not assumptions. The data sources and synthesis process differ from conventional UX research because AI behaviour is probabilistic and context-sensitive.
Captures over-trust drift and error habituation
Exposes explainability gaps and trust mis-calibration
Identifies when users abandon vs. override AI output
Maps harm tolerance to domain responsibility
Validates literacy level distribution across roles
Aim for 3–6 distinct clusters per product. More clusters add sprint overhead without proportionally improving coverage; fewer risk masking important subgroups like vulnerable or adversarial users.
Personas are most valuable when they directly generate acceptance-criteria (AC) language in sprint tickets. Each GenAI dimension maps to a testable AC pattern.
GIVEN a result, THEN plain-language rationale must be visible without extra clicks
"Summarise this contract" shows a highlighted risk list, not raw JSON
GIVEN the AI is wrong, THEN a confidence indicator below 70 % must be displayed prominently
Legal search shows amber badge with 'Verify before relying'
GIVEN any hallucination, THEN the user must be prompted to review source documents
Inline citation required for every factual claim
GIVEN a high-stakes domain action, THEN a human review step must gate the workflow
Contract clause change triggers approval queue
GIVEN a recommendation, THEN the top-3 contributing factors must be attributable
Decision page shows feature importances or chain-of-thought summary
GIVEN flagged persona segment, THEN content safeguards and referral links must activate
A cross-persona matrix ensures the product handles the full spread of user profiles without regressing for any segment. Run the matrix on every major feature that touches AI output.
Automate the matrix as a parametrised test suite. Inject synthetic persona profiles into the product's session context and assert each cell's expected behaviour using UI integration tests.
Persona Integration in the Development Workflow
Personas only create value when referenced during development, not just during discovery. The integration points below prevent personas from becoming shelf documents.
Each ticket lists which personas it must satisfy. Tickets with no persona reference are returned for annotation before estimation.
Tickets must include at least one GenAI-specific AC derived from persona attributes (literacy, trust, harm) before entering a sprint.
Demos include at least two contrasting personas (e.g., Literacy 1 + Literacy 4) to surface behavioural divergence in front of stakeholders.
Teams flag persona gaps revealed during the sprint. New insights feed back to the persona file within one sprint cycle.

Commit the persona registry to the same repository as the codebase. Version-controlling personas alongside code means pull requests can reference persona IDs, and reviewers can see when a change may affect a specific user segment.
AI user personas extend the standard persona format with five GenAI-specific dimensions that directly drive product, UX, and safety decisions:
determines UI complexity, onboarding depth, and what controls to expose.
determines what guardrails and confidence signals are necessary.
determines how much friction to add around high-stakes AI actions and how critical it is to minimise false positives.
determines what reasoning metadata must be surfaced and how.
determine the minimum required guardrail intensity and whether mandatory human review gates are necessary.
Personas without these dimensions produce features that serve the average user adequately and the edge-case user badly — and in GenAI, the edge-case user is disproportionately likely to be harmed.
